speaker: interviewer
text: OK, go ahead.

speaker: interviewer
text: Today is Wednesday, March 12, 1997. I'm interviewing Fred Manus. My name is Gayle Tallman. We are doing this interview in Edmonton, Alberta, Canada, and the language is English.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, we're rollling.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your name today?

speaker: interviewee
text: My present name is Manusowicz. M-A-- Should I spell it?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: M-A-N-U-S-O-W-I-C-Z.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what name do you go by today?

speaker: interviewee
text: Manus.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: M-A-N-U-S.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Manus, what is your birth date?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1930, 7th of October.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old are you today?

speaker: interviewee
text: Today? 67. Nearly.

speaker: interviewer
text: What city were you born in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Werba.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: W-E-R-B-A.

speaker: interviewer
text: And which is the largest town closest to Werba?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the largest would be Rowno.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: R-O-W-N-O.

speaker: interviewer
text: And which country were you born in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who are the members of your family? Start with your father.

speaker: interviewee
text: Our family consisted-- the father's name was Nathan. You want me to spell it as well? No? And the mother's name, Nechama. Then I'll start for the oldest brother, that was Shlomo. Second brother, [? Etsach. ?] And my name is Fred. Then we had a little baby sister was born in 1939, was Faiga. Faigele. Faiga. Like a bird, Faiga.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Do you remember what year your father was born in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Approximately, I would say 1897.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1897, approximately.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your eldest brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Also approximately, 1925 or 1926.

speaker: interviewer
text: And [? Etsach? ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Etsach, ?] 1928. And the little girl was born '39.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe the Jewish community in which you grew up in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we were a small town of about a little under 100 Jewish families, which probably was half of the population of the town. And majority of the Jewish population, they were tailors. Is it OK to-- Shoemakers, carpenters, they were mostly tradesmen. And also, some small businesses.

speaker: interviewer
text: What type of organizations existed?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, like in any other nationality, probably, there was-- I couldn't tell you, I was probably too young-- but there was some probably belonged to Communist party. But the majority of our Jewish population was Zionists. Maybe three different phrases of Zionist, like Betar and then, um, what's the-- labor. Jewish labor. But all connected with Zionists.

speaker: interviewer
text: What types of religious institutions did you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we had, uh, shul and the Beth Midrash. In other words, two Jewish synagogues. And we also had, in the synagogue, a Hebrew school for children. So we had two Jewish teachers in that-- in our little town.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a difference between the synagogue and the Beth Midrash?

speaker: interviewee
text: Beth Midrash. Well, not really. Maybe the Beth Midrash was a little more religious, but not much.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the Hebrew school was an afternoon school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was split. For example, if I went to public school in the morning, then I went in the afternoon to the Hebrew school. Or vice versa. Like, um, in our town, we only had one public school. So therefore, there was two different sessions. The older kids used to go first thing in the morning, and the younger ones used to go later in the afternoon, according to the-- to the darkness of the-- the darkness of the day. So if I went to morning public school, then afternoons I went to Hebrew school.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did your family support itself?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we had a little shoe store. Very little. It-- it was in the house. In them days, when somebody wanted to make a pair of shoes, they went to the shoemaker. The shoemaker told him what size, and my father used to cut the materials, sell it to the guy, and he took it back to the shoemaker to make a pair of shoes. Also, my father was traveling the farms. He was a grain buyer for a company. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] also, by him traveling the farms, in the summer holidays he used to take the kids with him. So we used to travel all over the country, the farms. And that helped us survive after, by knowing the country so well, by traveling all the farms, the woods. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: When your father was traveling, who else worked in the store?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, mother would be looking after it. And in a small town, if they-- in them days, if they needed somebody, they waited. Father always came home the same day.

speaker: interviewer
text: To which Jewish organizations did your family belong?

speaker: interviewee
text: I couldn't tell you. We were all dreaming of Zionism. But which? I would say, probably, he was close more with the Labor movement.

speaker: interviewer
text: What languages did you speak at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: None well, but probably Jewish was the main language and, uh, at home.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which would be Yiddish today.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yiddish, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What languages do you speak today?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, probably my second language would be English, and Ukrainian, and Russian.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your family religious?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not fanatically religious. We were considered [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] conservative. But we were very kosher in the house. Observed. Very kosher place.

speaker: interviewer
text: What other rituals did you observe? Did you observe the Sabbath?

speaker: interviewee
text: We went every day to synagogue. Not us kids, but the father went every day. We went every Sabbath and Friday night.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your whole family?

speaker: interviewee
text: The whole family. Mother-- in them days, women didn't go as much to synagogue. Mostly on holidays.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember if the women sat with the men?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, separately, on the balcony.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what we would probably consider Orthodox today.

speaker: interviewee
text: In them days, everybody was nearly Orthodox, kind of. It-- every 99% of our town, we were observing the Kashrut, which is kosher food. And probably 90% of the men went to synagogue every day.

speaker: interviewer
text: What type of school did you attend?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I started school in-- when I was 5 and 1/2, in public school. That was Polish school. And then I attended the pri-- the Hebrew school.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the afternoons?

speaker: interviewee
text: Or vice versa. Half-- half day split, or maybe the Hebrew school was a little shorter.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you learn in the Hebrew school? Just the language?

speaker: interviewee
text: Like our town was always-- they were educating us towards immigrating to Palestine. So Hebrew was a first, a must. And then it was religion, like the, uh-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Well, let's say religion. And how to write Hebrew, and how to speak Hebrew, and-- and how to pray, and--

speaker: interviewer
text: As a child, what type of activities did you like to do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it's-- my childhood didn't come about. But for whatever I had up until-- we played. Like-- we were studying quite a bit of the day. And-- but we did have a little activities, like playing maybe soccer, volleyball, and other athletic things. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you want to be when you grew up?

speaker: interviewee
text: I really couldn't tell you, because, uh-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The parents pushed us towards education. Every-- and mainly in Hebrew education. So-- but I actually-- maybe engineering would have been the closest thing that I was connected with.

speaker: interviewer
text: How would you describe the relationship between the Jews and the non-Jews of your town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it wasn't the worst, but it-- we as Jews, we associated mostly with Jewish kids. Very little that we played with other nationalities. And, uh, it wasn't easy for a Jewish kid to walk on the street. You were harassed, and-- not always, not by everybody, but we were harassed. So we were always sticking together, between the Jewish kids.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why would they harass you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because my name was Ephraim, and Jewish-- being Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you experience any anti-Semitism in school? In the public school you went to?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. Not openly, but quite a bit.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you receive it from the teachers as well as the children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really, no. Not from the teachers, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did Hitler's rise to power in 1933 affect your life?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1933, it didn't affect me. I was only three years old. And all I can tell you is what I read, which is probably-- it didn't affect in 1933, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you know about the Nazis?

speaker: interviewee
text: After. [PAUSES] When the Nazis--

speaker: interviewer
text: When you started to hear  about them?

speaker: interviewee
text: When we started hearing about them, we were very worried and very scared. And, uh, we were very happy when the Soviet Union invaded our country and we fell under their government instead of the German government.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what year was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1939 until 1941. So that saved us an extra two years of-- of hardship.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the response of your town, with the Soviets coming in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we were scared of the Soviets also. Because the Soviets, if-- majority of Jewish people, they couldn't own land. So they were in business, and the Soviets were against the Jewish people that were in business. They sent them to Siberia. Our name was on the list to go to Siberia later on. So we would've been lucky if we would have gone to Siberia. We would have survived, the whole family. So-- But we weren't too comfortable with the Soviets, because every day we were in the central rail. Our town was on-- on the central rail lines. And every day, there was Jewish business people with families transported to Siberia. My mother always used to give me a little bag of food. Take it to the train, and give it to them people. We felt sorry for them. But as it worked out, that's the only people that were lucky. They survived by sending them to Siberia. But at that time, we were not too comfortable with the Soviets, neither.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your family make any attempt to leave the country?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father tried to run away when the Germans were coming closer to our village, but it was too late. The Soviets informed us that they were taking the Germans, they're winning the war. They didn't tell us that the Germans are next door already. So the propaganda was that they're winning. And so we didn't-- the Germans came in so quickly, we didn't have a chance to-- to run away.

speaker: interviewer
text: And once they arrived?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Germans? That's when hell started.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I would-- should we start from the beginning? That will take me two hours.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's OK. Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] When the Germans came in, the first thing that I can remember-- I was 11 years old, approximately-- and the first hardship they did is every Jew had to wear a yellow badge on the front, and one in the back. They shamed us in front of the Gentile people walking the street. We were recognized. And secondly, they took us out of school. A Jew couldn't go to public school. Couldn't go to any school. We went-- under hiding, we went to the Hebrew school, which it was in no longer in a synagogue, but was in a private house of the-- of the teacher. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Then-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] The Germans was in from '41. The war started in '41, in about June. And they were there until March '44. And, uh-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] There was lots of different things that went through, like our parents told us that there's going to be hardships. And you were scared to walk on the street when the Germans came in. We were always hiding in the house. And after that, they put us in the ghetto. It wasn't a fenced in ghetto. It was a ghetto. They put us-- they took two Jewish streets and packed in all the Jews from the whole town into that ghetto. Also, they brought in other Jews from the smaller villages or farms, brought them into that ghetto. So things were cramped, very, very cramped.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the ghetto have a name?

speaker: interviewee
text: No name. Werba ghetto, that's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you come and go from the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: We could, because there was no fences. But after the Germans came in, they set up a local militia. They were pro-Nazi. Pro-Nazi, and, uh, and, uh, in some cases, they were worse than the Nazis. Because they knew the Jewish population. They knew them by first name, by name, by last name. And they were a little harder on us than the Nazis. Also the parents, I can remember, they pointed out to us that if you see Gestapo-- that was an elite German Nazi troop-- and they were-- and the Gestapo is the one that used to come and kill the Jews. So they pointed out to us, if you see Gestapo, run.

speaker: interviewer
text: When the local Polish population guarded you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Local Polish population, they were in the same position as us. It's the Ukrainian. This is Ukraine, and when the Nazis come in, the Poles weren't any longer. It was Ukrainian Nazi supporters.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did they do to the Polish community from your town?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were-- they were not treated well, but treated much better than the Jews. They weren't killed, but they were, um, mistreated in a way. Because the Polish government, they were ruling the Ukrainians, and then the Ukrainians started ruling and they mistreated the Poles. Vice versa.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were guarded by the Ukrainians, were you allowed to leave the ghetto as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were, because there was no fences. It-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We were-- Father used to-- it wasn't very strict that you couldn't get out. Like, Father used to go out to the farms, sneak out and bring some food. Trade some stuff for food.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it difficult if he had stayed in Werba to find food?

speaker: interviewee
text: It-- yes. You had to go to the farms.

speaker: interviewer
text: So other people would sometimes also go out to the farms?

speaker: interviewee
text: Other people, too, yeah. Because the Jewish stores, they-- they closed. So you couldn't buy any flour or any-- any everyday needs.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you live in an apartment in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We didn't have apartments, none of them. So, houses.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who lived in the house with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I really don't remember. In our original house or in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewer
text: In the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the ghetto. It was-- it was so cramped. And they set up the ghetto, they tried to [PAUSES] trick us, the Nazis, you know. So they set up the ghettos, working people ghetto on one side. The other side was business, or-- or one side was tradesmen, the other side was-- And like the parents figured that they wouldn't touch the tradesmen. If they do anything, they'll touch first the, uh, the business. So we stayed in the tradesmen section of the ghetto. And it was very cramped, like they brought in the extra-- the whole town into two streets, plus the-- from the surrounding district. So it was-- could have been 10 in a room, 12 in a room.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have ration cards? Or did they not provide any food?

speaker: interviewee
text: Rations, they didn't provide. You were on your own.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your father work when you were in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Couldn't work, no. A Jew lost all his privileges when the Nazis came in. You couldn't-- they did take the work, they-- for Nazi type of work, which is things that weren't pleasant.
